dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 for suspected sepsis at --Address Redacted-- in the city of Richmond.
Hello ma'am, I'm David, a paramedic with EMS. I see you're seated in your wheelchair. Could you tell me your name?
Yes, she's having trouble with her thoughts and memory. She's been like this since this morning, maybe around 8 a.m.
Can you tell me your name, please?
My name's Mary... it's Mary.
Where are we now, ma'am?
I think we're in my living room...
Is it comfortable for you to sit up like this, or should we adjust your wheelchair for better support?
Let me check your breathing, ma'am. Your breathing is a bit fast, around 18 breaths per minute. Is it hard for you to breathe at all?
Pulse is 74, strong and regular. Skin is pink, warm, and dry. Looks good so far.
She's been acting this way since this morning. She was released from the hospital two days ago after pneumonia. They prescribed some antibiotics, but she said she's not feeling any better.
Does she have a history of COPD or hypertension?
Yes, she has COPD and primary hypertension. She also recently completed a course of antibiotics and had a negative COVID test.
Ma'am, when was the last time you had something to eat or drink?
She eats light, but had breakfast today.
What happened this morning to bring on these symptoms?
She just sat down and started to mumble a bit. Her nurse thought it was strange.
Pulse is 74 - looks good, clear and consistent.
Respiratory rate remains 18. Still stable.
Blood pressure is 136 over 58. Slightly low, but within acceptable range.
Oxygen saturation is at 99. You're doing great on that.
Blood sugar initially was 176. We'll monitor closely for changes.
You have a history of COPD; are you experiencing wheezing or shortness of breath with this altered status?
No, her breathing hasn't changed significantly. Rales were noticed in her lung.
Skin looks good-no rashes, no bruising, and no bites that I see.
You seem okay on head and neck exam, no signs of rigidity or cervical pain.
The FAST exam showed no face droop, arm drift, or slurred speech-so no signs of stroke.
Your lungs do have some rales and rhonchi. That's not uncommon with a history of pneumonia. Is this normal for you?
I don't see any wounds or swelling. Your legs and arms look normal.
EKG is normal, just sinus. No signs of trouble in the rhythm.
We are placing you on a 4-lead EKG for monitoring. Do you mind if we secure this on your chest?
Now placing the full 12-lead. It might feel a bit cold.
Let me print this out for further evaluation at the hospital.

dialogue.micro_intent: iv start - extremity vein (arm or leg)
dialogue.utterance: Ma'am, we are going to try to start an IV in your hand. It may pinch for a moment.

dialogue.micro_intent: iv start - extremity vein (arm or leg)
dialogue.utterance: Clinically, that was unsuccessful. We'll continue monitoring without fluids at this time.
We are proceeding according to protocol for medical - altered mental status based on her symptoms and vitals.
Pulse just hit 77, and we're seeing some increased respiratory rate again-28 per minute.
Blood pressure down to 124 over 62. Oxygen saturation 98. That's not terrible.
Heart rhythm still normal. EKG again routine.
Let's get the most recent vitals now to track trends.
Pulse unchanged at 77. Respiratory rate dropped back to 11 recently.
BP now 114 over 56, still acceptable. Oxygen is at 96.
Let's check these readings for accuracy one more time.
Pulse increased slightly to 80, otherwise all good signs so far.
Respiratory rate is up again, 27. Monitoring is ongoing.
Per the EPCR, she is not experiencing pain at this time.
Ma'am, we'd like to transport you to St. Mary's for further evaluation. Is that okay with you and the family?
Securing you in the wheelchair and onto the stretcher. You'll be calm during the transfer.
I'll call ahead to the hospital regarding the sepsis alert.
I'll handle the radio contact and pass along care notes to the facility on our approach.
Transport is now stable, and the patient is doing as best as can be expected.
An RN signature will be obtained upon arrival due to her altered mental status, as she is unable to sign.
The condition is consistent with sepsis. EKG findings and vital sign instability are at the doctor's discretion for hospital care.
All equipment has been cleared from the scene prior to departure. Final check complete.
We will handle the head-to-toe movement and secure the patient closely during transport.
Stationing the stretcher into the ambulance now. Ready for departure.
She's never been this out-of-it before. We're grateful for your help.
Thank you for your honesty. We observed an AMS and are calling ahead for sepsis evaluation.
Let me assist with the stretcher positioning into the ambulance.
We're finished on-scene. Moving to transport position now.
I just passed along confirmation to the ER ahead of us.
We are moving along a safe and direct route to the hospital now.
You're amazing. She's sleeping now, oddly calm.
That's good to hear. We expect her to improve with hospital intervention.
I've got securing lines locked down. Watching vitals closely.
ER on the line confirmed arrival time. All clear.
Hospital secured our arrival time and met us at the doors.
The patient is stable for movement. We're transferring to the ER on foot with sheet move.
Transfer complete and also handed off to the ER staff.
All EPC documentation passed to the ER as a final handoff.
Handoff complete in the EMR.
RN was signed upon arrival as per hospital protocol due to her altered mental status.
All remaining documentation and PPE handling done and packed as per protocol.
Sign-out complete. EMS resources freed as official patient absorption.
Most recent BP was 110 over 55. We haven't noted deterioration but we'll continue to monitor that.
